title: A Survey of Pun Generation: Datasets, Evaluations and Methodologies
research_questions: What datasets have been used and constructed for pun research, and how can they be categorized into generic, derived, and human-annotated datasets?
What methodologies have been used for pun generation across conventional methods, classic deep neural networks, pre-trained language models, prompting, and visual-language models?
What automatic and human evaluation metrics are used to assess pun generation quality?
What challenges and promising future directions remain for pun generation, especially in multilingual, multimodal, and prompting-based settings?

methods_proposed.name: Pun generation survey taxonomy
methods_proposed.summary: The survey organizes pun generation research into datasets, methods, and evaluation, and classifies methods along a technological timeline: conventional/template methods, classic DNNs, PLM fine-tuning, PLM prompting, and visual-language models.

humor_theories.detail: The paper reviews surprisal as an automatic evaluation metric that quantifies surprise based on the pun word and its alternative word under local and global contexts.
The survey grounds pun categories in linguistic ambiguity, including polysemy, homophony, heterography, homography, and visual double meaning.

evaluation_methods.detail: The survey summarizes ambiguity, distinctiveness, surprisal, unusualness, Dist-1 & Dist-2, perplexity score, and structure success as automatic evaluation metrics.
The survey summarizes human evaluation metrics including success, funniness, fluency, informativeness, coherence, and readability, often assessed with Likert scales or binary success judgments.
The survey notes that Zeng et al. (2024) replaced human reviewers with GPT-4 to assess readability, funniness, and coherence.
The survey describes SemEval as manually or expert annotated and notes later human-annotated datasets such as ExPUNations, CUP, ChinesePun, and UNPIE.

judges.detail: This is a non-empirical survey and does not conduct original human or model judging experiments.

generation_selection.detail: The paper does not generate new pun outputs; it reviews prior pun generation work.

inference_budgets.detail: No inference was run by the survey paper.

human_baselines.detail: No original model evaluation or matched human baseline is reported.

contamination_risk.basis: The paper is a literature survey and does not evaluate models on benchmark data.

llm_judge_dependence.detail: The survey mentions GPT-4 judging in prior work, but its own conclusions are not based on LLM-as-judge evaluation.

reporting_gaps: The paper collection procedure gives approximate counts, such as “around 150 publications” and “approximately 30 papers,” but does not report query dates, full search strings, or detailed inclusion/exclusion criteria.
The survey does not provide a quantitative meta-analysis of model performance across papers.
The paper itself notes that many prior human evaluation studies fail to provide detailed information about evaluators’ backgrounds.
No original model provenance, inference budgets, or generation-selection procedures are applicable because the paper runs no experiments.

headline_results.finding: The paper collection process initially identified around 150 publications and then filtered to approximately 30 papers focused on pun generation.
The survey lists SemEval as an expert-sourced English homographic and heterographic pun dataset with 2,878 corpus items.
The survey lists ExPUNations as a SemEval-derived English homographic and heterographic pun dataset with 1,999 corpus items.
The survey lists CUP as a SemEval-derived English homographic and heterographic pun dataset with 2,396 corpus items.
The survey lists ChinesePun as a Chinese homophonic and homographic pun dataset with 2,106 corpus items, split into ChinesePun-P with 1,049 and ChinesePun-G with 1,057.
The survey lists UNPIE as a multi-language homographic and heterographic pun dataset with 1,000 corpus items, split into UNPIE-P with 500 and UNPIE-G with 500.

key_findings: The survey presents pun generation as a creative NLG task centered on semantic ambiguity, phonetic similarity, coherence, and contextual appropriateness.

It categorizes pun datasets into generic datasets, derived datasets, and human-annotated datasets, and identifies SemEval as the first expert-annotated pun dataset and one of the most commonly used in the pun generation community.

It organizes methods into conventional template systems, classic DNNs, PLM fine-tuning, PLM prompting, and visual-language models, describing a shift from manually engineered systems to LLM-based approaches.

The survey argues that human evaluation is essential for pun generation, while automatic metrics such as perplexity, diversity, surprisal, ambiguity, and structure success capture only parts of pun quality.
The paper highlights that most pun generation research focuses on English, while multilingual and multimodal pun generation remain underexplored.
The survey states that LLMs show potential for pun generation but still exhibit limitations in creativity and humour, including a “Lazy Pun Generation” pattern noted in recent studies.
limitations: The authors state that some works may have been missed due to variations in search keywords.
The authors state that their exploration of other categories of puns is limited, including recursive puns and antanaclasis.
The authors state that the study does not cover the entire historical scope nor the latest advancements following the survey because of rapid development in the field.

The paper notes that automatic evaluation metrics have limitations: surprisal is context-dependent, Dist-1 and Dist-2 fail to capture semantic diversity, and perplexity does not necessarily imply semantic coherence or logical consistency.
The paper notes that human evaluation is subjective and affected by participants’ cultural backgrounds and knowledge levels, and that many studies do not report detailed evaluator backgrounds.
